title: AI Can't Replace Human Therapy
igFbThreads:
Human psychology is immensely complex, nuanced, and often contradictory, with significant portions operating outside conscious awareness. AI, even advanced language models, operates on algorithms and data. While it can process language, it lacks true intuition, lived experience, and the capacity to grasp the intricate nature of human emotional life.

At PsiAN, we believe that therapies of depth, insight, and relationship aim to understand and resolve the underlying psychological conflicts and patterns that give rise to symptoms. The goal is not just symptom reduction but profound personal growth and healthier ways of relating to self and others.

Join PsiAN today if you share these values.

#HumanTherapy #DepthPsychology #MentalHealthMatters #PsiAN

title: AI Therapy Risks - Unconscious

Considering AI for mental health support? Be aware of the limitations, especially when it comes to accessing the unconscious mind.

AI can mimic empathy, but it cannot truly understand the complex, often hidden motivations and conflicts that drive our feelings. This can lead to superficial "solutions" and potentially dangerous responses during crises.

Without a human therapist to navigate these dynamics, vital insights and true relational healing are missed. And ethical concerns over data privacy remain significant.

Learn more about what leads to lasting therapeutic change at PsiAN.org

title: Mental Health Apps Privacy
igFbThreads:
Mental health apps: Convenient, but are they enough?

Technology can assist, but apps cannot fully replace the nuanced connection of human-to-human therapy. While they may be useful tools for some purposes, apps are not replacements for therapists.

Many mental health apps also operate outside of HIPAA regulations, giving them more leeway in handling user data. Reports indicate that numerous apps collect and share sensitive information—including mental health symptoms, personal details, and usage patterns—with data brokers and advertisers.

The drive for profit can overshadow user privacy and ethical considerations. Let's use tech wisely.

#mentalhealth #therapy #tech #mentalhealthawareness #digitalhealth #PsiAN

**Link**: https://www.brookings.edu/articles/why-mental-health-apps-need-to-take-privacy-more-seriously/

title: Rome Wasn't Built in a Day
igFbThreads:
"Rome wasn't built in a day." "Old habits die hard." "All in good time."

The wisdom of these sayings is obvious—so why wouldn't they apply to therapy as well?

It's understandable to want a quick fix to psychological problems. No one wants to prolong their distress. But quick fixes work for the moment, not the long haul.

At PsiAN, we advocate for depth psychology: treatment that examines early experiences and how they impact your current behavior, thoughts, feelings, and relationships. Understanding how persistent patterns came to be—and working through how they affect your life—is the most effective path to lasting change.

They say Rome wasn't built in a day. You weren't built in a day either. It takes time to achieve mental wellness, but it's time well spent.

Learn more at PsiAN.org

title: Gold Standard Misinformation Thread
igFbThreads:
The term "gold standard" in therapy is often misleading—and it primarily benefits insurance companies at the expense of patients.

Recent research titled "More treatment but no less depression: The treatment-prevalence paradox" describes a puzzling finding: increased availability of short-term therapies hasn't reduced depression rates.

This isn't surprising to depth therapists who understand that relational problems developed over years or decades require more than a few weeks to meaningfully address.

The researchers themselves acknowledge that benefits from short-term treatments are often modest, rarely sustained, and don't translate well to real-world settings.

Therapies of depth, insight, and relationship address deeper emotional patterns, fostering lasting transformation. Research shows patients in these therapies continue improving for years after treatment ends.

Choosing depth-oriented therapy means investing in meaningful, enduring emotional wellbeing—not just temporary relief.

#TherapyThatSticks #depthpsychology #mentalhealth #PsiAN
linkedin:
The term "gold standard" in therapy research deserves scrutiny—and understanding who benefits from promoting it.

Recent research titled "More treatment but no less depression: The treatment-prevalence paradox" (Ormel et al., 2022) highlights a significant finding: despite increased availability of short-term, symptom-focused treatments, depression prevalence hasn't decreased.

This paradox isn't surprising to depth-oriented clinicians. Relational problems and patterns that developed over years or decades logically require more than a few weeks to meaningfully address.

Notably, the study authors—some of whom advocate for short-term methods—acknowledge that benefits from these treatments are often modest, rarely sustained, and don't generalize well to real-world clinical settings.

The "gold standard" label often obscures a more complex reality: while brief interventions may produce measurable short-term effects in controlled trials, therapies of depth, insight, and relationship produce change that compounds over time. Research consistently shows patients continue improving for years after such treatment ends.

The question isn't which approach produces faster results—it's which produces lasting ones.

title: Apps & Mental Health - Long Post
igFbThreads:
Thinking about using a therapy app? Here's what you should know before taking that step:

1. Apps like BetterHelp and TalkSpace are tech companies first, mental health practices second. Their priority is platform expansion; mental health is the product.

2. "24/7 therapist access" sounds appealing, but research shows it's not therapeutic. Consistency—same person, same time, same place—creates the safety essential for real therapeutic work.

3. Effective psychotherapy relies on two things: a consistent relationship with and trust in your therapist. That's difficult with anonymous, potentially changing "therapists."

4. These platforms have the potential to share your data with third parties to serve their growth. Your struggles may become their business asset.

5. App-based therapy is limited in what it can address. They're not equipped for serious mental health crises, and sometimes operate contrary to best practices.

6. Research supports the efficacy of long-term, relationship-based psychotherapy. Most people seeking therapy want a lasting solution with an in-person therapist.

If you're ready to start therapy with a safe, consistent, effective approach, visit PsiAN.org for resources.

#therapy #mentalhealth #therapyapps #PsiAN

**Links**: Multiple research citations available
linkedin:
Before choosing a therapy app, consider these important factors:

**1. Business model clarity**: Platforms like BetterHelp and TalkSpace are primarily technology companies. Their core directive is market expansion; mental health services are the product offering.

**2. The "24/7 access" myth**: While constant availability sounds appealing, research indicates it's not therapeutically beneficial. Effective therapy relies on consistency—same clinician, regular scheduling—which creates the psychological safety necessary for meaningful work.

**3. The relationship foundation**: Effective psychotherapy depends on two elements: a consistent therapeutic relationship and trust in your therapist. These are difficult to establish with anonymous, potentially rotating providers.

**4. Data considerations**: These platforms may share user data with third parties to support growth objectives. Sensitive mental health information deserves stronger protections.

**5. Scope limitations**: App-based services are limited in the conditions they address and aren't equipped for serious mental health crises. Some practices may contradict clinical best practices.

**6. What research shows**: Evidence supports the efficacy of long-term, relationship-based psychotherapy. Our research indicates most people seeking therapy want lasting solutions with consistent, in-person treatment.

If you're beginning your therapy journey and want guidance on finding quality care, visit PsiAN.org

title: Dr. Powell-Williams Spotlight (DATE REMOVED)
igFbThreads:
We're spotlighting Dr. Felecia Powell-Williams—a powerhouse in psychotherapy and PsiAN Board Member.

As a distinguished psychoanalyst, Dr. Powell-Williams champions depth, insight, and the profound importance of the therapeutic relationship in healing. She recognized early in her career the "limitations of short-term treatment" and the need for "deeper, more sustained therapeutic relationships."

title: Depth Therapy Threads Post 1 (David, 8/12)
igFbThreads:
Therapies of depth, insight, and relationship start with a simple, human premise: our most troubling beliefs once protected us. They were solutions to real conditions, often in family or developmental contexts.

Instead of debating those beliefs in the present, we ask: "When did they begin? With whom? What did they help you survive?" This lens invites compassion and curiosity rather than shame.

In a steady therapeutic relationship, these answers are felt and experienced—something far more powerful than cognitive understanding alone. Old expectations are reworked in real time, offering new experiences of safety, repair, and choice.

Insight grows from this process: the old belief made sense, so it no longer needs harsh judgment. And it no longer has to run the show. That relief opens the door to lasting change.

#depthpsychology #therapy #mentalhealth #insight #PsiAN

title: Depth Therapy Threads Post 2 (David, 8/12)
igFbThreads:
Skill-building and quick fixes can be helpful. But therapy of depth, insight, and relationship asks a different question: Why do the same patterns keep returning? We look for the story behind the symptom.

Patterns are relational. They formed with important others, often in our families, and they inevitably appear in our other relationships too. Noticing them together—in the here-and-now—turns therapy into a living laboratory.

Our relationships are so powerful in shaping our patterns that it makes sense to practice a therapy that is itself relational. Research shows the relational aspect of therapy produces meaningful outcome differences.

The changes that happen in a therapeutic relationship get internalized as direct counterweights to earlier relational harm. Patients live the experience that things can be different and better.

Change that is understood from the inside tends to endure. It's often slower at first, but it sticks—because it's rooted in lived experience, not just new thoughts or skills.

#therapy #depthpsychology #relational #mentalhealth #PsiAN

title: Depth Therapy Threads Post 3 (David, 8/12)
igFbThreads:
What does therapy of depth, insight, and relationship look like in practice?

We slow down. We track and experience emotion as deeply as possible. We listen for meanings held in the body and in memory—especially during early relationships, which tend to be the most formative.

We make room for the disavowed. We all show parts of ourselves and hide others, sometimes even from ourselves. When we hide parts in shame, we become less whole. Depth therapy attempts to reclaim these losses.

We find reasons for symptoms and patterns—usually dating back years or decades, often originating as ways to survive difficult circumstances. This understanding helps release their grip.

We pay close attention to the relationship unfolding in therapy. How we show, hide, laugh, anger, ignore, move toward, pull away—these illuminate who we are as social beings.

An attuned therapist helps us see these dynamics unfold, explores them with curiosity, and works toward healthier ways of relating—often healing old wounds in the process.

Therapy of depth is one of the hardest and most rewarding things you can do.

#depthpsychology #therapy #healing #mentalhealth #PsiAN

**We attend to the therapeutic relationship itself.** How we show, hide, approach, withdraw—these dynamics illuminate our relational patterns in real time. An attuned therapist notices these patterns, explores them with curiosity, and facilitates healthier ways of relating.

This work is among the most challenging and rewarding endeavors a person can undertake. If this approach resonates with you, learn more at PsiAN.org.

title: Insurance Hassle Post (Ben, 10/9)
igFbThreads:
The Hidden Toll of "Taking Insurance" in Therapy

It sounds simple: pay for therapy with your insurance. But for many dedicated therapists, working with insurance companies is an administrative burden that takes time away from patient care.

The hassle: Hours spent fighting for authorization, mountains of claim forms, and reimbursement rates that barely cover practice costs.

The control: Invasive demands for private patient information and "medical necessity" requirements that dictate how and how long you can receive care—often overriding clinical judgment.

This is why many therapists choose to be self-pay only. It's not about greed; it's about protecting their energy to focus entirely on you and provide ethical care free from corporate interference.

Therapy should be about healing, not paperwork and profits.

#SelfPayTherapy #TherapyTruth #MentalHealthAdvocate #PsiAN

title: Insurance Thread (David, 10/21)
igFbThreads:
Insurance in mental health: the good, the bad, and the ugly.

The good: Coverage can increase access to care for people who otherwise couldn't afford it.

The bad: Low reimbursement rates, prior authorization requirements that drain clinical time, ghost networks listing unavailable providers, and frequent claim denials.

The ugly: Insurers effectively dictate patient care—shorter sessions, capped frequency, pressure to assign diagnoses for billing purposes rather than clinical accuracy.

Treatments get interrupted or delayed. Documentation requirements consume hours. Clinical judgment gets sidelined by utilization rules. Companies intentionally make themselves difficult to work with, hoping providers give up on collecting payment.

PsiAN wants to make these profit-driven problems more visible. Use our Insurance Issue Tracker to log denials, delays, clawbacks, and credentialing issues. We're building collective power against these practices.

#insurance #mentalhealth #therapy #advocacy #PsiAN

**The good:** Insurance coverage can meaningfully increase access for people who couldn't otherwise afford care.

**The bad:** Low reimbursement rates, prior authorization requirements consuming clinical time, ghost networks listing unavailable providers, and routine claim denials create substantial barriers.

**The concerning:** Insurers increasingly influence clinical decisions—mandating session length, capping frequency, creating pressure to assign diagnoses for billing rather than clinical accuracy.

The downstream effects: treatment interruptions and delays, documentation requirements consuming hours that should go to patient care, clinical judgment overridden by utilization management protocols. Some industry practices appear designed to discourage providers from pursuing legitimate reimbursement.

At PsiAN, we're working to document these systemic issues and build collective leverage for change. Our Insurance Issue Tracker collects data on denials, delays, clawbacks, and credentialing problems.

If you've experienced these issues, your data point matters: https://www.psian.org/insurance-issue-tracker

title: Self-Pay Thread (David, 10/21)
igFbThreads:
Self-pay therapy is a difficult decision for both clinicians and patients.

For clinicians, it's a weighing of reach versus the ability to practice responsibly: protect privacy, set session length and frequency based on need, maintain continuity, and prevent treatment from being shaped by insurer rules.

Therapists choose self-pay to protect patients from profit-focused insurers: fewer administrative hurdles, sustainable rates that allow time for extra patient support, less pressure to diagnose for payment purposes, and reduced data sharing.

If you're a patient, you may still be able to make self-pay work. During open enrollment, check your out-of-network benefits, deductibles, and HSA/FSA options. Ask potential therapists about sliding scale fees and superbills.

Plan ahead with your therapist to estimate frequency and duration, so you can budget accurately.

#therapy #selfpay #mentalhealth #PsiAN

title: Advocacy Power
igFbThreads:
Amplify the Silence: The Power of Advocacy in Mental Health

Mental health access isn't just a clinical issue—it's a societal one.

As therapists and allies, advocacy extends our work beyond the therapy room. It means actively working to ensure everyone can access quality care, regardless of systemic barriers.

Why is advocacy critical now?

Addressing systemic barriers: When policies restrict funding, limit coverage, or create deserts of care, they compound individual suffering. Advocacy addresses the structural side of mental health.

Ensuring access and equity: We must speak up against policies that create barriers. Advocating for thoughtful, human-centered policy is essential to closing the mental health gap.

Advocacy isn't partisan—it's caring for collective wellbeing. Join PsiAN today to help build a more just and supportive mental health landscape.

#MentalHealthAdvocacy #Psychotherapy #MentalHealthAccess #PsiAN

title: Mental Health Awareness Month Kickoff
igFbThreads:
This Mental Health Awareness Month, we're talking about more than awareness. We're talking about access to therapy that actually helps people heal.

For too long, the public conversation about mental health has been dominated by quick fixes, app-based "solutions," and short-term symptom management. But the research and the public agree on what works: therapies of depth, insight, and relationship. In PsiAN's 2025 national study, 90 percent of Americans surveyed said they want therapy that helps them get to the root of their problems, not just manage symptoms.

This May, PsiAN is sharing what depth therapy looks like, who is fighting for it, and how you can join us in protecting access to meaningful psychotherapy.

Stay with us all month. Learn more at psian.org.

#MentalHealthAwarenessMonth #PsiAN #DepthTherapy #TherapyThatWorks

title: 4 Percent — NIMH Funding Priorities
igFbThreads:
4 percent.

That is the share of the National Institute of Mental Health's budget that goes to psychotherapy research, according to peer-reviewed research recently published by a team including PsiAN co-founder Linda Michaels alongside Al Galves, Patrick Hahn, William Foreman, Karen Zilberstein, and Melissa Cole.

Where does the rest go? Approximately 75 percent funds basic research on the brain, physiology, and genetics. Treatment research overall receives just 14 percent. And within that small psychotherapy slice, the vast majority is directed toward cognitive-behavioral models. Therapies that emphasize depth, insight, and the therapeutic relationship, with substantial evidence for effectiveness and long-term benefit, receive virtually no federal support.

Former NIMH Director Tom Insel has estimated that approximately 20 billion dollars has been spent on neuroscientific research at NIMH. That investment has not led to reductions in suicide rates, psychiatric hospitalizations, or the global burden of mental illness.

#NIMH #MentalHealthResearch #PsiAN #DepthTherapy

**Link**: https://www.psian.org/blog/new-research-examines-national-institute-of-mental-health-funding-priorities
linkedin:
A new analysis of National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH) funding priorities, recently published in two peer-reviewed papers by a team including PsiAN co-founder Linda Michaels, surfaces a striking imbalance.

- Approximately 75 percent of the NIMH budget funds basic research on the brain, physiology, and genetics
- 14 percent goes to treatment research overall
- Only 4 percent supports psychotherapy research
- Within that 4 percent, the vast majority funds cognitive-behavioral models. Depth, insight, and relationship-oriented therapies, despite substantial evidence for long-term effectiveness, receive virtually no federal support

The research team — Karen Zilberstein, MSW; Al Galves, PhD; Melissa Cole, MA; William Foreman, PhD; Patrick Hahn, MA; and Linda Michaels, PsyD, MBA — conducted a painstaking analysis of NIMH-funded projects to better understand where federal mental health research dollars are going, and what that means for the field and for the people we serve.

Former NIMH Director Tom Insel has estimated that approximately 20 billion dollars in neuroscientific research at NIMH has not led to measurable reductions in suicide rates, psychiatric hospitalizations, or the overall global burden of mental illness. The disconnect between funding priorities and clinical impact is increasingly difficult to ignore.

title: PsiAN Urges Caution on AI in Clinical Care
igFbThreads:
AI medical tools are not ready for accelerated adoption in clinical care without meaningful regulation and a robust evidence base.

That was PsiAN's central message in our public comment submitted to the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services in response to its Request for Information on accelerating AI adoption in clinical care.

Our comment urges federal policymakers to prioritize patient safety, privacy, clinical efficacy, and enforceable regulation, particularly in behavioral health and psychotherapy. We highlighted the absence of a comprehensive federal regulatory framework, the lack of HIPAA protections for many mental health AI products, and the documented cases of AI systems providing harmful guidance to vulnerable individuals, including those experiencing suicidal ideation.

Without enforceable federal oversight, corporations lack strong incentives to prioritize patient protection over profitability.

Read the full PsiAN comment.

#PsiAN #AIRegulation #MentalHealthAdvocacy

**Link**: https://www.psian.org/blog/psychotherapy-action-network-urges-caution-on-ai-in-clinical-care-in-federal-comments-to-hhs
linkedin:
AI medical tools are not ready for accelerated adoption in clinical care without meaningful regulation and a robust evidence base.

That is the central message in PsiAN's public comment submitted to the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services in response to its Request for Information (RIN 0955–AA13) regarding accelerating the adoption and use of artificial intelligence in clinical care.

Representing more than 6,500 members and 90 partner organizations, PsiAN urges federal policymakers to:

- Establish uniform national standards for privacy and accountability before expanding AI use in healthcare
- Support rigorous, independent research on AI safety and efficacy, conducted by independent researchers rather than the corporations developing and selling the products
- Collaborate with clinicians and psychotherapy researchers to define appropriate clinical metrics
- Await FDA determinations on whether AI tools qualify as medical devices before accelerating adoption

Decades of psychotherapy research confirm that the therapist-patient relationship is the strongest predictor of treatment outcome. AI systems, regardless of sophistication, do not constitute human relationships. PsiAN raises a critical policy question: Should AI function as a support tool for licensed clinicians, an administrative aid, or as a replacement for psychotherapy?

title: Enlarging the Frame — A Special Issue in Tribute to Erika Schmidt
igFbThreads:
PsiAN is proud to announce the publication of "Enlarging the Frame, Expanding Our Worlds: The Many Uses of Depth Therapy," Volume 46, Issue 1 of Psychoanalytic Inquiry.

This special issue is a tribute to Erika Schmidt, who was part of PsiAN from the beginning and most recently served as our vice chair. Erika was a pioneer many times over: the first woman, first social worker, first non-MD, and first child analyst to become president of the Chicago Psychoanalytic Institute. She died unexpectedly in late 2022, a premature loss to so many.

Before her death, Erika posed the generative question that shaped this volume: How is depth therapy used in different ways beyond traditional one-on-one adult treatment? The articles, contributed by current and former PsiAN board members, demonstrate the breadth of contemporary depth psychotherapy across psychosis, marginalized college students, organizational leadership, cultural analysis, ethics, and mental health parity law.

#PsiAN #DepthTherapy #ErikaSchmidt #PsychoanalyticInquiry

The volume is dedicated to Erika Schmidt, who was part of PsiAN from the beginning and most recently served as our vice chair. title: Confidentiality Cannot Be a Compliance Checkbox
igFbThreads:
"Confidentiality is not merely a legal obligation; it is a core condition that allows patients to speak freely without fear."
— Linda Michaels, "Holding the Frame in an Era of Corporate Care"

In PsiAN's recent practice management company (PMC) study, 81 percent of clinicians using PMCs reported concern that these platforms do not adequately protect patient information. Therapists expressed anxiety about data tracking, third-party data sharing, and the use of clinical material for purposes unrelated to care, such as training artificial intelligence systems.

Headway, one of the largest PMCs, is currently the subject of a class action lawsuit alleging that it allowed Google's tracking technology to collect sensitive mental health data without adequate consent. While the case is ongoing and Headway denies the accusation, the allegations illustrate broader concerns about data privacy in digital mental health platforms.

When therapists or patients believe clinical material may be tracked, monetized, or repurposed, the symbolic safety of the therapeutic frame is compromised.

#PsiAN #DataPrivacy #PMCs

**Link**: https://americanpsychoanalyst.substack.com/p/holding-the-frame-in-an-era-of-corporate

title: Where the Other 96 Percent Goes
igFbThreads:
If only 4 percent of the National Institute of Mental Health's budget goes to psychotherapy research, where does the rest go?

According to peer-reviewed analysis by Galves, Hahn, Foreman, Zilberstein, Michaels, and Cole, in 2020:
- Approximately 75 percent funded basic research on the brain, physiology, and genetics
- 14 percent funded treatment research overall (including the 4 percent for psychotherapy)
- The remainder funded other categories

The trend is also worth noting. Between 2012 and 2020, the share of NIMH funding for brain and genetics research rose from 71 percent to 74 percent. Treatment research declined from 20 percent to 14 percent. Psychotherapy specifically dropped from 7 percent to 4 percent.

When the funding model decreasingly studies what helps people in real-world clinical settings, the field's ability to advance treatment suffers.

Read the full PsiAN announcement and access both peer-reviewed articles.

#NIMH #MentalHealthResearch #PsiAN

**Link**: https://www.psian.org/blog/new-research-examines-national-institute-of-mental-health-funding-priorities
linkedin:
If only 4 percent of the National Institute of Mental Health's budget goes to psychotherapy research, where does the rest go?

According to peer-reviewed analysis by Galves, Hahn, Foreman, Zilberstein, Michaels, and Cole, published in Ethical Human Psychology and Psychiatry, in 2020:

- Approximately 75 percent funded basic research on the brain, physiology, and genetics
- 14 percent funded treatment research overall (including the 4 percent for psychotherapy)

The trend matters as much as the snapshot. Between 2012 and 2020:

- Funding for brain and genetics research rose from 71 percent to 74 percent of the NIMH budget
- Treatment research declined from 20 percent to 14 percent
- Psychotherapy research specifically dropped from 7 percent to 4 percent

And within that already small psychotherapy slice, when both 2012 and 2020 funding are combined, cognitive-behavioral therapy received the largest share at 44 percent, followed by behavioral therapy (18 percent), family therapy (11 percent), and solution-focused therapy (8 percent). Therapies of depth, insight, and relationship received virtually nothing.

When the funding model decreasingly studies what helps people in real-world clinical settings, the field's ability to advance treatment, and to challenge the dominance of any single approach, suffers.

title: Closing Mental Health Awareness Month
igFbThreads:
As Mental Health Awareness Month closes, the work continues.

Throughout May we have shared the research and the advocacy that defines PsiAN's work: the Mad in America interview with our co-founder Linda Michaels, the new analysis of National Institute of Mental Health funding priorities, our practice management company study, the federal AI comments, the insurance advocacy, and the special issue of Psychoanalytic Inquiry tributing Erika Schmidt.

The thread that connects all of these is simple. People deserve therapy that actually helps them heal, not symptom management dressed up as therapy. Therapists deserve the conditions to do that work, not corporate intermediaries that take a cut and degrade the frame. Public policy and federal funding should align with what people want and what works.

Awareness is necessary. It is not enough.

This summer we are continuing to share PsiAN's work, our partner organizations, our board and advisor leaders, and the research and advocacy that move the field forward. Stay with us.

title: 84 Percent Did Not Know How They Were Being Paid
igFbThreads:
84 percent.

That is the share of therapists using practice management companies (PMCs) who told PsiAN they did not know how the reimbursements from insurers were split between them and the platform.

In a traditional group practice, fee-splitting calculations are common knowledge. In a PMC, the financial terms are not. Many therapists only learned the details after they joined.

The promise PMCs make is that therapists will earn more by going through the platform than they could on their own. PsiAN's research found only half of therapists actually earned more. The other half earned the same or less. Many described the experience as a bait-and-switch.

In psychotherapy, money is not only a transaction but a symbolic signal of value, responsibility, expectations, and mutual commitment. Nontransparent fee structures are not a minor inconvenience.

#PsiAN #PracticeManagementCompanies #TherapistAdvocacy

**Link**: https://www.psian.org/practice-management-companies
linkedin:
84 percent.

That is the share of therapists using practice management companies (PMCs) who told PsiAN they did not know how the reimbursements from insurers were split between them and the platform.

In a traditional group practice, fee-splitting calculations are common knowledge. In a PMC, the financial terms are not. Many therapists only learned the details after they joined.

The promise PMCs make is that therapists will earn more by going through the platform than they could on their own, because the platform can negotiate better rates with insurers using the leverage of thousands of contracted clinicians. PsiAN's research found that only half of therapists actually earned more after joining a PMC. The other half earned the same or less. Many described the experience as a bait and switch.

As Linda Michaels writes in The American Psychoanalyst: "In psychotherapy, money is not only a transaction but a symbolic signal of value, responsibility, expectations, and mutual commitment. Nontransparent fee structures and shifting payment terms distort this economic frame, introducing confusion and undermining therapists' capacity to hold stable boundaries around time, frequency, and care. This is also a clear failure of informed consent for therapists and indirectly for patients."

title: AI Without an Evidence Base
igFbThreads:
"Corporate metrics such as user engagement, downloads, and customer satisfaction are not substitutes for rigorous clinical outcome data."
— from PsiAN's federal comment to HHS on AI in clinical care

Large language models, while technologically advanced, are prone to generating inaccurate or fabricated outputs. Documented cases show AI systems providing harmful guidance to vulnerable individuals, including those experiencing suicidal ideation.

New AI tools should be evaluated using meaningful health metrics, including symptom remission, dropout rates, sustained recovery, and improvements in quality of life. And evaluation should be conducted by independent researchers, not by the corporations developing and selling the products. The conflicts of interest are otherwise too significant to ignore.

Until that evidence exists, accelerated adoption is premature.

#PsiAN #AIRegulation #MentalHealthCare

**Link**: https://www.psian.org/blog/psychotherapy-action-network-urges-caution-on-ai-in-clinical-care-in-federal-comments-to-hhs
linkedin:
"Corporate metrics such as user engagement, downloads, and customer satisfaction are not substitutes for rigorous clinical outcome data."
— from PsiAN's federal comment to HHS on AI in clinical care

Large language models, while technologically advanced, are prone to generating inaccurate or fabricated outputs. Documented cases show AI systems providing harmful guidance to vulnerable individuals, including those experiencing suicidal ideation.

New AI tools deployed in mental healthcare should be evaluated using meaningful health metrics: symptom remission, dropout rates, sustained recovery, improvements in quality of life. The Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration defines recovery as a long-term, self-directed process leading to improved health and meaningful life participation. AI systems should be evaluated against these broader, recovery-oriented outcomes, not simply short-term symptom change or app engagement.

And evaluation should be conducted by independent researchers, not by the corporations developing and selling the products. The conflicts of interest are otherwise too significant to ignore.

Until rigorous, independent evidence exists, accelerated adoption is premature.

title: Depth Therapy for Psychosis
igFbThreads:
"The trauma that predisposes to psychosis is relational trauma. A relational solution is an essential element in treatment that cannot be obtained from a pharmaceutical molecule."
— Michael Garrett, MD

In the special issue of Psychoanalytic Inquiry tributing Erika Schmidt, Dr. Garrett challenges the prevailing assumption that depth therapy approaches are neither useful nor indicated for those with psychosis. Although the standard recommended treatment is often antipsychotic medication, Garrett summarizes the research showing that early abuse experiences often lead to psychosis later in life.

A depth, relational approach can help make meaning of psychotic experience, which Garrett views as essential to healing. He also issues a call for clinicians to participate in the front lines of public psychiatry, where the needs are substantial and many patients suffer from the lack of trusting relationships that a psychoanalytic perspective can offer.

#PsiAN #DepthTherapy #Psychosis #PsychoanalyticInquiry

**Link**: https://www.psian.org/blog/enlarging-the-frame-expanding-our-worlds-the-many-uses-of-depth-therapy

Although the standard recommended treatment is often antipsychotic medication, Garrett provides an overview of research showing that early abuse experiences often lead to psychosis later in life.

His article includes a clinical transcript that illuminates how a depth, relational approach can help make meaning of psychotic experience, which he views as essential to healing. Garrett also issues a call for clinicians to participate in the front lines of public psychiatry, where not only are the needs substantial, but many patients suffer from the lack of trusting relationships that a psychoanalytic perspective can offer.

This is one example of how depth therapy is being used beyond the traditional one-on-one adult treatment model — a question that shaped the entire special issue.

title: Partner Spotlight — Austen Riggs Center
igFbThreads:
Partner spotlight: Austen Riggs Center

Nestled in the Berkshires in Stockbridge, Massachusetts, the Austen Riggs Center is a residential psychiatric treatment community that treats the person, not just the diagnosis.

Founded in 1913, Riggs offers an open and voluntary continuum of care for adults with complex psychiatric problems. All patients receive intensive individual psychodynamic psychotherapy four times a week, alongside a robust Therapeutic Community Program in which patients develop supportive peer relationships.

In a treatment landscape increasingly dominated by short, manualized, symptom-focused interventions, Riggs has held the line on what depth, relational psychiatric treatment can be. We are honored to count Austen Riggs among our partner organizations.

#PartnerSpotlight #PsiAN #AustenRiggs #DepthTherapy

title: Bait and Switch — Continuity of Care Under PMCs
igFbThreads:
"When care is repeatedly threatened with oversight or interruption, psychotherapy can shift away from deeper, exploratory work and toward crisis management."
— Linda Michaels, "Holding the Frame in an Era of Corporate Care"

Practice management companies (PMCs) regularly change session limits, reimbursement terms, and payment models after therapists sign up. These shifts destabilize the basic continuity that makes psychotherapy effective.

title: $20 Billion Without Reducing the Burden
igFbThreads:
Approximately $20 billion. That is what former National Institute of Mental Health Director Tom Insel has estimated has been spent on neuroscientific research at NIMH.

That investment has not led to reductions in suicide rates. It has not led to reductions in psychiatric hospitalizations. It has not reduced the overall global burden of mental illness.

This is not an indictment of brain or genetic research, which can be valuable. It is an indictment of the imbalance. By framing mental illness primarily as a brain disorder, current funding priorities overlook the powerful role of social context, lived experience, relationships, and environmental factors in mental health. These are precisely the dimensions that depth-oriented and relational psychotherapies are designed to address.

Aligning research investments with what actually helps people offers our best chance of meeting the mental health needs of those who are suffering now.

#PsiAN #NIMH #MentalHealthResearch

**Link**: https://www.psian.org/blog/new-research-examines-national-institute-of-mental-health-funding-priorities

title: The Mega Group Practice Model
igFbThreads:
Practice management companies (PMCs) operate as "mega group practices," where therapists are 1099 independent contractors to the platform.

The arrangement is similar to a rideshare platform like Uber or Lyft: drivers are technically self-employed but are deeply beholden to the structure and incentives of the platform.

This raises ethical and legal questions that traditional group practice models do not. Who owns the patient data? What happens if a therapist wants to leave? Who decides what impacts client care?

These mega groups also offer few of the benefits of a traditional group practice. There is no supportive network of colleagues, no office, no health insurance plan. Therapists work for the platform — without the legal rights of an employee — as opposed to the platform working for them.

#PsiAN #PracticeManagementCompanies #TherapistAdvocacy

**Link**: https://americanpsychoanalyst.substack.com/p/holding-the-frame-in-an-era-of-corporate

title: Depth Therapy and the Case of Asian Americans
igFbThreads:
"Psychoanalysis derided consideration of sociocultural factors — cultural values, class, gender, ethnicity, race, and race-related dynamics — as mere sociology."
— Kris Yi, PhD

In the Erika Schmidt tribute issue of Psychoanalytic Inquiry, Dr. Yi challenges two intertwined myths: that psychoanalytic therapy is inappropriate for Asian Americans, and that CBT is the treatment of choice (despite the lack of empirical evidence for this claim).

Yi traces these myths to psychoanalysis's own Western bias and its universalizing theories — which applied a Western construction of self across all cultures, even collectivist ones — at a time when CBT was adopting a more culturally sensitive approach.

But psychoanalysis and the consideration of individual subjectivity and trauma can be powerfully healing for Asian Americans raised to subsume their experiences to the collective. The myths are inherited, not justified.

#PsiAN #DepthTherapy #PsychoanalyticInquiry

**Link**: https://www.psian.org/blog/enlarging-the-frame-expanding-our-worlds-the-many-uses-of-depth-therapy
linkedin:

Yi traces these myths to psychoanalysis's own Western bias and its universalizing theories, which applied a Western construction of self across all cultures, even collectivist ones, at a time when CBT was adopting a more culturally sensitive approach. Interestingly, the dissociations of early immigrant psychoanalysts fleeing Europe in WWII mirror those of some Asians amidst the mass traumas they experienced.

Yi shows how psychoanalysis and the consideration of individual subjectivity and trauma can be powerfully healing for Asian Americans raised to subsume their experiences to the collective. The myths are inherited, not justified.

This is part of the special issue exploring depth therapy beyond the traditional one-on-one adult treatment model.

title: The Strongest Predictor of Treatment Outcome
igFbThreads:
Decades of psychotherapy research confirm that the therapist-patient relationship is the strongest predictor of treatment outcome.

Not the modality. Not the manual. Not the technique. The relationship.

AI systems, regardless of sophistication, do not constitute human relationships. They cannot bear witness. They cannot hold ambivalence. They cannot register the moment when a patient tells the truth about something they have never said out loud. They process language. They do not have a stake in another person's becoming.

This is why PsiAN's federal comment to HHS asks the critical policy question: Should AI function as a support tool for licensed clinicians, an administrative aid, or as a replacement for psychotherapy? At present, there is insufficient evidence to justify AI as a substitute for trained mental health professionals.

#PsiAN #TherapeuticRelationship #AIRegulation

**Link**: https://www.psian.org/blog/psychotherapy-action-network-urges-caution-on-ai-in-clinical-care-in-federal-comments-to-hhs

title: Partner Spotlight — Access Institute
igFbThreads:
Partner spotlight: Access Institute

Founded in 2002, Access Institute is San Francisco's mental health safety net — a nonprofit providing high-quality, intensive psychotherapy to low-income, culturally diverse Bay Area residents. Sliding-scale fees start at $30 per session.

Access provides individual psychotherapy, couple and marital therapy, family therapy, and play therapy for children, alongside specialized programs for youth and seniors. Their therapists are graduate and post-graduate interns supervised by licensed clinicians, trained in psychoanalytic and community-service-focused practice.

Through community partnerships, Access also works with children, families, and teachers in seven public elementary schools in San Francisco and with older adults at the Bayview Hunter's Point Adult Day Health Center.

Access is a model of what depth-oriented community mental health can look like.

title: Capitation by Another Name
igFbThreads:
Many practice management companies (PMCs) are moving toward a new payment model in which therapists are no longer paid by the session or by the hour, but in lump sums.

It is now called "value-based payment." It is a rebranded version of capitation.

Under capitation, the platform receives a fixed amount per patient and the therapist absorbs the financial risk of treatment that runs longer than expected, requires more sessions, or involves more complexity. The risk that historically sat with insurers shifts to individual clinicians.

The newer language sounds reform-oriented. The mechanics are not. As insurers and PMC platforms push this model, therapists should know what they are signing up for.

#PsiAN #PracticeManagementCompanies #TherapistAdvocacy

**Link**: https://americanpsychoanalyst.substack.com/p/holding-the-frame-in-an-era-of-corporate
linkedin:
Many practice management companies (PMCs) are moving toward a new payment model in which therapists are no longer paid by the session or by the hour, but in lump sums.

Under capitation, the platform receives a fixed amount per patient and the individual clinician absorbs the financial risk of treatment that runs longer than expected, requires more sessions, or involves more complexity. The risk that historically sat with insurers shifts to therapists, who have far less ability to bear it.

The newer language sounds reform-oriented. The mechanics are not. Capitation has a long and well-documented history in healthcare, including the powerful incentive it creates to limit care. As insurers and PMC platforms push this model in mental healthcare, therapists deserve to know what they are signing up for and how it differs from fee-for-service arrangements.

This is one of the developments PsiAN's PMC research and Linda Michaels's TAP article track in detail.

title: Mental Health Parity as an Advocacy Tool
igFbThreads:
"The for-profit health insurance industry is ill-prepared to handle the soaring demand for care, primarily by discriminating against mental health services."
— D. Brian Hufford, Esq.

In the Erika Schmidt tribute issue of Psychoanalytic Inquiry, attorney D. Brian Hufford makes the case for mental health parity law as a tool to challenge inadequate insurance networks and low reimbursement rates.

Insurers prioritize shareholders by underpaying mental health clinicians and failing to maintain adequate networks, often using "ghost networks" — directories that list providers who are unavailable, no longer practicing, or not actually accepting new patients. The result: patients are pushed to more expensive out-of-network care or forego treatment entirely.

Amidst federal regulatory retreats and the limitations of state laws, Hufford stresses that continued advocacy and activism are necessary. PsiAN's Insurance Hub gives clinicians the tools to participate.

#PsiAN #MentalHealthParity #InsuranceAdvocacy

**Link**: https://www.psian.org/blog/enlarging-the-frame-expanding-our-worlds-the-many-uses-of-depth-therapy

Founded in Chicago in 1901 by Jane Addams and her colleagues, JPA brings compassionate, innovative mental health resources directly to children in need and the adults who support them.

Over 97 percent of JPA's young clients are African American or Latinx, qualify for free or reduced-price lunch, and live in communities where violence is rampant.

JPA is showing what depth-informed, community-embedded child mental health care can do.

title: Symptom Checklists Sent Without Consent
igFbThreads:
Several practice management companies have started sending symptom checklists directly to patients — without asking or informing the therapist, let alone obtaining clinical consent.

This is not a small thing. The PMC has become a third party in the therapeutic relationship, contacting the patient on its own schedule, collecting data on its own initiative, and shaping the patient's experience of care without the clinician's involvement.

In PsiAN's research, the majority of patients report that their goals are to get to the root of their issues and increase self-awareness — goals deeper and broader than tracking discrete levels of symptomatology. Symptom checklists imposed from outside the therapy can undermine those goals.

The trust and authority earned by the therapist, granted by the patient, is at stake.

#PsiAN #PracticeManagementCompanies #ClinicalAutonomy

**Link**: https://americanpsychoanalyst.substack.com/p/holding-the-frame-in-an-era-of-corporate
linkedin:
Several practice management companies (PMCs) have started sending symptom checklists directly to patients — without asking or informing the therapist, let alone obtaining clinical consent.

This direct intrusion of PMCs into the therapist-patient relationship is not only frame-breaking, it is threatening to privacy, ethics, and the clinician's authority. The PMC has become a third party in the therapeutic relationship, contacting the patient on its own schedule, collecting data on its own initiative, and shaping the patient's experience of care without the clinician's involvement.

There are also serious questions about what these checklists measure and how. In PsiAN's research, the majority of patients said their goals were to get to the root of their issues and increase self-awareness — goals that are deeper and broader than assessing discrete levels of symptomatology. Symptom checklists imposed from outside the therapy can shift the focus of treatment in directions that don't align with what either the patient or the clinician is working toward.

The trust and authority earned by the therapist, granted by the patient, is at risk — paving the way for enactments around control and, in some cases, premature termination of treatment.

title: Support, Aid, or Replace? The Critical AI Question
igFbThreads:
PsiAN's federal comment to HHS posed a question that policy on AI in mental healthcare must answer:

Should AI function as a support tool for licensed clinicians? As an administrative aid? Or as a replacement for psychotherapy?

The three possibilities are not interchangeable. Each implies a different regulatory framework, a different evidence base, a different role for human clinicians, and a different relationship to patients. Treating them as if they were the same — or assuming the answer can be deferred — is how harmful policy gets made by default.

Policymakers are seeking solutions to persistent gaps in mental healthcare access. AI is often proposed as a potential solution. But widespread deployment without adequate safeguards can introduce new harms while leaving access problems unsolved.

#PsiAN #AIRegulation #MentalHealthAdvocacy

**Link**: https://www.psian.org/blog/psychotherapy-action-network-urges-caution-on-ai-in-clinical-care-in-federal-comments-to-hhs

title: A Call for Rebalancing
igFbThreads:
The PsiAN-affiliated research on National Institute of Mental Health funding priorities provides a valuable data set that can be used to advocate for a more balanced approach to mental health research funding — one that considers the whole human being, not just biological and neurological components.

A rebalancing of priorities could:
- Support treatments grounded in lived experience, relational dynamics, and real-world effectiveness
- Make space for research on therapies of depth, insight, and relationship that have been systematically underfunded
- Align federal investment with what people actually want from therapy and what produces lasting change

Aligning research investment with what helps people offers our best chance of meeting the mental health needs of those who are suffering now.

#PsiAN #NIMH #MentalHealthResearch

**Link**: https://www.psian.org/blog/new-research-examines-national-institute-of-mental-health-funding-priorities
linkedin:

- Support treatments grounded in lived experience, relational dynamics, and real-world effectiveness
- Make space for research on therapies of depth, insight, and relationship that have been systematically underfunded
- Align federal investment with what people consistently say they want from therapy
- Move us closer to interventions that produce lasting change rather than short-term symptom reduction

For psychotherapists, researchers, advocates, and the public alike, this matters deeply. Systematic underfunding of clinically relevant research risks slowing progress toward person-centered, effective treatments at exactly the moment when psychological and emotional distress are widespread and urgent.

Aligning research investment with what actually helps people offers our best chance of meeting the mental health needs of those who are suffering now.

title: Partner Spotlight — The Kedzie Center
igFbThreads:
Partner spotlight: The Kedzie Center

The Kedzie Center is the first community-funded mental health center in the city of Chicago, serving the North River community under the Illinois Community Mental Health Services Act of 2011. The center is psychodynamically informed and was created through an innovative strategy for funding urban mental health services.

The Kedzie Center provides individual, family, and group therapy, psychiatric consultation, and guidance in identifying and accessing community resources. Services are offered in both English and Spanish to children, adolescents, and adults.

The center also trains a small cohort of advanced graduate and doctoral students in psychodynamic therapy in the community — modeling how the next generation of clinicians can be prepared to serve underserved neighborhoods.

title: If They Can Do It for PMCs, They Can Do It for Everyone
igFbThreads:
Insurers have shown — through practice management companies — that they can change their many discriminatory practices.

PMCs offer credentialing within 60 days, not the six months insurers typically take. Reimbursement rates can be higher. Payments to therapists can occur on a regular, predictable basis.

These should not be exclusive features available only to therapists who agree to become contractors to insurer-owned platforms. They should be offered to all therapists, immediately.

If the insurer can credential a PMC contractor in 60 days, the insurer can credential an independent therapist in 60 days. If the insurer can pay a PMC reliably, the insurer can pay a therapist reliably. The fact that these basic conditions are being used as a carrot to drive therapists into PMC arrangements is the point.

#PsiAN #InsuranceAdvocacy #TherapistAdvocacy

**Link**: https://americanpsychoanalyst.substack.com/p/holding-the-frame-in-an-era-of-corporate
linkedin:
Insurers have shown — through their financial relationships with practice management companies — that they can change their many discriminatory practices when it suits them.

PMCs typically offer:
- Credentialing within 60 days, rather than the six months insurers commonly take with independent clinicians
- Higher reimbursement rates than individual therapists can negotiate on their own
- Reliable, predictable payment cycles

These should not be exclusive features available only to therapists who agree to become contractors to insurer-owned platforms. As Linda Michaels argues in The American Psychoanalyst, "These 'revolutionary features' should be offered to all therapists immediately, not only those who join a PMC."

If the insurer can credential a PMC contractor in 60 days, the insurer can credential an independent therapist in 60 days. If the insurer can pay a PMC reliably, the insurer can pay a therapist reliably. The fact that these basic conditions are being used as a carrot to drive therapists into PMC arrangements — where insurers gain greater control and therapists lose autonomy — is precisely the point.

The constructive ask is straightforward. The harder question is who has the standing to demand it. PsiAN's view: collective advocacy, including legislative and regulatory action, is required.

Read Linda's full article: https://americanpsychoanalyst.substack.com/p/holding-the-frame-in-an-era-of-corporate

title: Why Therapists Go Self-Pay
igFbThreads:
"Therapists are paid less than other clinicians, burdened with extra paperwork, and forced to justify treatment in ways other providers often are not. It is just not right."
— Linda Michaels in Mad in America

It is understandable that many therapists move toward self-pay practices, even though that decision makes care more expensive and less accessible to patients without resources. But this is not a therapist problem. It is an insurance industry problem.

Insurance companies discriminate against mental health. Therapists are paid roughly 20 percent less than comparable clinicians in the medical/surgical field. Combined with excessive administrative demands, the rational choice for many is to leave the panel.

The result: fewer in-network options, higher out-of-network costs for patients, and drastically reduced access to therapy for the public.

The problem is the system. The solution is collective advocacy.

#PsiAN #InsuranceAdvocacy #SelfPay

**Link**: https://www.madinamerica.com/2026/04/the-fight-for-the-soul-of-psychotherapy-an-interview-with-linda-michaels/
linkedin:

Insurance companies discriminate against mental health. Therapists are paid roughly 20 percent less than comparable clinicians in the medical/surgical field. Combined with excessive administrative demands and the threat of audits and clawbacks, the rational choice for many clinicians is to leave the insurance panel.

The result is what we see: fewer in-network options, higher out-of-network costs for patients, and drastically reduced access to psychotherapy for the public.

The burdens of this system get projected onto individual therapists (who feel guilty about going self-pay) and individual patients (who blame themselves for not finding affordable care). But the problem is structural. The solution is collective advocacy: legislative action, regulatory enforcement, and public pressure for parity.

title: Depth Therapy for First-Generation, Low-Income College Students
igFbThreads:
First-generation, low-income college students are often strangers in a strange land — straddling their culture of origin as they encounter the new culture, norms, language, and expectations of college.

In the Erika Schmidt tribute issue of Psychoanalytic Inquiry, Adam Rodriguez writes about therapy with these young adults, often people of color, navigating multiple intersecting transitions and subjectivities. They need help mourning losses as they move away from family and culture, while simultaneously encountering new demands without the support, resources, and preparation of their non-FGLI peers.

Rodriguez deepens the consideration of upward mobility and pursuit of the "American dream," highlighting the complexity, ambivalence, and numerous losses inherent in that experience. In intimate, poignant case studies, he describes how a depth approach uniquely helps his patients while developing a powerful, loving, and deeply empathic relationship with each of them.

#PsiAN #DepthTherapy #PsychoanalyticInquiry

**Link**: https://www.psian.org/blog/enlarging-the-frame-expanding-our-worlds-the-many-uses-of-depth-therapy

First-generation, low-income (FGLI) college students are often strangers in a strange land — straddling their culture of origin as they encounter the new culture, norms, language, and expectations of college.

In the Erika Schmidt tribute issue of Psychoanalytic Inquiry, Adam Rodriguez, PsyD, writes about therapy with these young adults — frequently people of color — navigating multiple intersecting transitions and subjectivities. His article, "Going for Depth While Striving to New Heights: Depth Therapy for First-Generation, Low-Income College Students," frames the FGLI experience in ways that resemble a cross-cultural immigration: guilt and internal conflicts are prominent, and a new bicultural identity is forged.

Rodriguez deepens the consideration of upward mobility and pursuit of the "American dream," highlighting the complexity, ambivalence, and numerous losses inherent in that experience. He shows how FGLI students need help mourning losses as they move away from family and culture, while simultaneously encountering new demands without the support, resources, and preparation of their non-FGLI peers.

In intimate, poignant case studies, Rodriguez describes how a depth approach uniquely helps his patients while developing a powerful, loving, and deeply empathic relationship with each of them.

This is one of the articles in PsiAN's special issue exploring depth therapy beyond the traditional one-on-one adult treatment model.

title: Get Out of the Way
igFbThreads:
"We do not need more middlemen and more administrators. We need them to get out of the way so we can safeguard clinicians' authority over treatment decisions."
— Linda Michaels, "Holding the Frame in an Era of Corporate Care"

If we are to improve access, outcomes, and clinician sustainability, attention must shift from solely what interventions are delivered to how therapy is structured. Psychotherapy cannot be separated from the conditions under which it occurs.

At a minimum, this means:
- Frequency and intensity of sessions based on clinical need, not corporate metric
- Confidentiality defended in substance, not limited to minimal compliance
- Transparency of financial matters, ownership, and payment structures
- Clinicians' authority over treatment decisions, recognized and protected

Protecting the psychotherapy frame is not about preserving tradition or privileging one orientation over another. It is about safeguarding the conditions that allow therapeutic relationships, of any modality, to do what they are meant to do: help people heal, grow, and change.

#PsiAN #PsychotherapyFrame #DepthTherapy

**Link**: https://americanpsychoanalyst.substack.com/p/holding-the-frame-in-an-era-of-corporate

- Confidentiality defended in substance, not limited to policies meeting minimal compliance
- Transparency of financial matters, ownership structures, and payment models

Clinicians, for their part, must educate themselves about new companies and trends in the field, reassert their authority as the party responsible for and in charge of the care provided, view business decisions as clinical decisions, and recognize the impacts and consequences of systemic intrusions into the frame.

Protecting the psychotherapy frame is not about preserving tradition or privileging one orientation over another. It is about safeguarding the conditions that allow therapeutic relationships — of any modality — to do what they are meant to do: help people heal, grow, and change.

Partner spotlight: Boston Graduate School of Psychoanalysis (BGSP)

Founded in 1973, BGSP is the only regionally accredited school of psychoanalytic studies in the United States to grant graduate degrees. The main campus is in Brookline, Massachusetts, with a New Jersey branch.

BGSP offers graduate degrees in psychoanalysis, mental health counseling, and socio-cultural studies, including a Doctor of Psychoanalysis (Psya.D.), a Master of Arts in Clinical Mental Health Counseling, and a Master of Arts in Psychoanalysis.

Notably, BGSP does not require GRE scores. Applicants interview with three faculty members instead — a small but telling sign of the depth-oriented values the program is built on.

We are honored to count BGSP among our partner organizations.

title: What Gets Funded Gets Taught
igFbThreads:
NIMH funding does not just determine what gets researched. It shapes what gets taught.

When 75 percent of federal mental health research dollars go to brain and genetics research, and only 4 percent goes to psychotherapy research (with depth, insight, and relationship therapies receiving virtually nothing), the downstream effects are predictable:

- Fewer graduate programs offer rigorous training in depth therapies
- Fewer faculty positions exist for researchers in those areas
- Fewer dissertations get written
- Fewer findings get published
- Fewer clinicians enter the field prepared to practice depth-oriented care
- Fewer patients have access to it

The funding pattern reproduces itself. Over decades, an entire family of effective therapies can be sidelined not because the evidence has changed, but because the structural support has been withdrawn.

This is why federal funding is an advocacy issue.

#PsiAN #NIMH #DepthTherapy

**Link**: https://www.psian.org/blog/new-research-examines-national-institute-of-mental-health-funding-priorities

- Fewer graduate programs offer rigorous training in depth therapies
- Fewer faculty positions exist for researchers and educators in those areas
- Fewer dissertations get written and fewer findings get published
- Fewer clinicians enter the field prepared to practice depth-oriented care
- Fewer patients have access to it

The funding pattern reproduces itself. Over decades, an entire family of effective therapies can be sidelined not because the evidence has changed, but because the structural support has been withdrawn. The myths that followed (that depth therapies are "old-fashioned," "not evidence-based," "less effective") track the funding, not the actual outcome data.

This is why federal funding is an advocacy issue, not just a research issue. The PsiAN-affiliated peer-reviewed papers on NIMH funding priorities give the field the data needed to make the case.

title: Partner Spotlight — A Home Within
igFbThreads:
Partner spotlight: A Home Within

A Home Within is the only national nonprofit focused exclusively on meeting the emotional needs of current and former foster youth. Their model is simple, radical, and rare: free, weekly, one-to-one psychotherapy with a licensed therapist, "for as long as it takes." There is no time limit on care.

A Home Within identifies, recruits, trains, and supports a network of licensed clinicians who provide this care free of charge. The organization currently has 19 chapters of volunteer clinicians across 10 states.

Research from the Foster Care Research Group at the University of San Francisco has shown that A Home Within's model of long-term, healthy relationships with foster care youth has led to statistically significant improvement in psychological and behavioral outcomes.

In a healthcare system that increasingly limits care to what insurance will pay for, A Home Within is a reminder of what depth-oriented work can do when it is structured around the patient.

#PartnerSpotlight #PsiAN #AHomeWithin

**Link**: https://www.ahomewithin.org/

title: Why Advocacy Matters
igFbThreads:
Mental health access is not just a clinical issue. It is a societal one.

For therapists and mental health advocates, our work extends beyond individual treatment. Advocacy means actively working to ensure that quality care is accessible regardless of socioeconomic status, geography, or systemic barriers.

PsiAN brings together clinicians, researchers, and advocates who believe that:

- Therapy that helps people heal at the root deserves to be defended
- The conditions that make therapy effective are themselves a public good
- Federal funding, insurance practices, and corporate behavior shape access more than any individual clinical decision
- Therapists working alone cannot fix structural problems, but therapists working together can

Almost 7,000 of us already have. There is room for more.

#PsiAN #MentalHealthAdvocacy #JoinUs

**Link**: https://www.psian.org/

title: 90 Percent — What People Want From Therapy
igFbThreads:
90 percent.

That is the share of Americans in PsiAN's national research who said they prefer therapy that helps them get to the root of their problems over a shorter approach focused mainly on symptom relief.

The number held in PsiAN's 2021 study (a representative sample of more than 1,500 Americans) and held again in our 2025 follow-up. People have not changed what they want.

The marketplace has simply gotten better at selling them something else.

When the public is asked directly, in their own words, what they value most in therapy, they describe depth, insight, and relationship. They want to make connections between past and present, understand patterns that drive their lives, and build a relationship with a therapist they can trust.

People know what works. The system should be aligned with that, not against it.

#PsiAN #DepthTherapy #PublicAttitudes

**Link**: https://www.psian.org/
linkedin:
90 percent.

That is the share of Americans in PsiAN's national research who said they prefer therapy that helps them get to the root of their problems over a shorter approach focused mainly on symptom relief.

The number held in PsiAN's 2021 study, conducted with a representative sample of more than 1,500 Americans, and held again in our 2025 follow-up. The pandemic, the explosion of telehealth, the rise of AI products, the proliferation of mental health apps — none of it changed what people want from therapy.

When the public is asked directly, in their own words, what they value most in therapy, they describe depth, insight, and relationship. They want to make connections between past and present, understand the patterns that drive their lives, and build a relationship with a therapist they can trust over time. They understand intuitively that real change takes work and time. They want it anyway.

The marketplace has gotten very good at selling them something else: short, manualized protocols, app-based engagement, AI chatbots, "5 stars or your money back" experiences. The mismatch between public preference and product offering is one of the central tensions PsiAN was founded to address.

People know what works. The system should be aligned with that, not against it.

The work continues.

This summer PsiAN has shared the research and advocacy that defines our mission: our federal comment to HHS on AI in clinical care, our peer-reviewed analysis of National Institute of Mental Health funding priorities, the Mad in America interview with our co-founder Linda Michaels, the practice management company study, the Insurance Hub, the Erika Schmidt special issue of Psychoanalytic Inquiry, our partner organizations, and the board and advisory leaders shaping this work.

Across all of it, the throughline has been simple. People deserve therapy that actually helps them heal, not symptom management dressed up as therapy. Therapists deserve the conditions to do that work, not corporate intermediaries that take a cut and degrade the therapeutic frame. Federal funding, insurance practices, and corporate behavior should align with both.

The forces working against that alignment are well-resourced, organized, and patient. They have spent years building infrastructure: practice management platforms, AI products, research funding patterns that crowd out depth, insurance arrangements that push therapists out of network. The forces working toward alignment (clinicians, researchers, advocates, partner organizations, and patients) are spread out, often working alone, and frequently under-resourced.

PsiAN exists to bring them together. The growth of our membership to nearly 7,000 mental health professionals, alongside more than 90 partner organizations, suggests the appetite for collective action is real and growing.

If you have followed along this summer, thank you. If you are ready to do more, we are ready for you.

Join PsiAN: https://www.psian.org/
